key_events: Post-1998 crisis stabilisation. NBU maintained a narrow band around 5.3–5.4 UAH/USD. Extensive FX market intervention. Monetary aggregates as nominal anchor (de jure), but exchange rate as de facto anchor.
sovereignty_rationale: Exchange rate anchor constrained monetary policy via impossible trinity. Interest rates subordinated to peg maintenance.

key_events: NBU officially revalued hryvnia to 5.05 UAH/USD on 21 Apr 2005 (from ~5.3). Rate held constant through strong capital inflows and commodity boom. Rapid credit growth and real estate bubble.
sovereignty_rationale: Rigid peg eliminated monetary policy independence. NBU accumulated reserves to sterilise capital inflows but could not control domestic credit expansion independently.

key_events: Global Financial Crisis. Steel export collapse, capital flight, banking crisis. IMF Stand-By Arrangement ($16.4bn, Nov 2008). NBU forced to abandon peg. Hryvnia fell from 5.05 to ~7.7 UAH/USD by early 2009.
sovereignty_rationale: The devaluation itself was an exercise of exchange-rate adjustment unavailable under EA membership. However, it was forced rather than chosen — a crisis response, not discretionary policy.

key_events: NBU re-established de facto peg at ~7.99 UAH/USD. Multiple IMF programs (SBA 2008, 2010, 2014). Gradual recovery then stagnation. Growing current account deficit. Political tensions building toward Euromaidan.
sovereignty_rationale: De facto dollar peg restored. NBU intervened heavily to maintain the rate, depleting reserves. Monetary policy again subordinated to exchange-rate target. Calvo & Reinhart (2002) 'fear of floating' diagnosis applies.

key_events: Euromaidan revolution (Feb 2014). Russian annexation of Crimea (Mar 2014). Armed conflict in Donbas (Apr 2014 onward). Massive capital flight, reserve depletion. NBU announced managed float (Feb 2014), then effective free fall. Rate collapsed from ~8 to ~33 UAH/USD. Severe banking crisis: 96 banks liquidated (2014–2017).
capital_controls: Severe (Feb 2015: mandatory FX surrender 75%, ban on dividend repatriation, cross-border transfer limits)
sovereignty_rationale: The devaluation was the primary macroeconomic adjustment mechanism. This is precisely the exchange-rate buffer that EA membership would eliminate. Without it, Ukraine would have required internal devaluation (wage/price deflation) — the 'Greek scenario'.

key_events: NBU adopted inflation targeting framework (Aug 2015 de jure, operational from 2016). Medium-term target: 5% ± 1pp. Key policy rate became primary instrument. Gradual disinflation from ~43% (2015) to ~2.7% (2020). IMF Extended Fund Facility (2015, 2020). Significant institutional reform of NBU.
capital_controls: Gradually eased (FX surrender reduced, capital account partially liberalised 2017–2019)
sovereignty_rationale: First period of genuine, discretionary monetary policy. NBU had operational independence, a clear nominal anchor (inflation target), and actively used the policy rate. This is the period where EA membership would represent the largest change in monetary framework — replacing an independent IT central bank with the ECB.

key_events: Full-scale Russian invasion (24 Feb 2022). NBU immediately fixed the rate at 29.25 UAH/USD. Emergency measures: policy rate raised from 10% to 25%. Martial law declared. Massive fiscal and monetary expansion to finance war effort.
capital_controls: Severe (ban on FX purchases by individuals, mandatory 100% FX surrender for exporters, cross-border transfer limits, cash withdrawal limits)
sovereignty_rationale: Wartime peg eliminated exchange-rate flexibility. Monetary policy subordinated to war financing needs. The decision to fix rather than float was itself an exercise of sovereignty, but once fixed, the regime resembled a conventional peg.

key_events: Stepped devaluation to 36.57 UAH/USD (21 Jul 2022, ≈25%). Gradual managed depreciation. NBU began cautious transition back to flexible exchange rate (Oct 2023). Capital controls gradually eased. IMF Extended Fund Facility ($15.6bn, Mar 2023). Rate reached ~41 by end 2024, ~44 by late 2025.
capital_controls: Gradually easing (FX surrender reduced to 50%, individual FX purchase limits raised)
sovereignty_rationale: Partial return to managed flexibility. NBU sets policy rate actively (reduced from 25% to 13% by mid-2024) but exchange rate management constrains full independence. Transition toward IT restoration ongoing.
